title: Vanilla marshmallows
subtitle: Gelatine-set marshmallows made with a hot sugar syrup and beaten egg whites
excerpt: Homemade marshmallows are light and well-flavoured, unlike the commercial foam kind. A hot sugar syrup at 127°C is poured into whipped gelatine and egg whites, then beaten until cool and thick. Set in a tin dusted with icing sugar and cornflour, cut into cubes, and tossed to coat.
sourceNotes: Marshmallows were originally made from the mallow plant root (Althaea officinalis), a medical and confectionery preparation documented from ancient times. The sugar-and-gelatine version using whipped egg whites appears in domestic confectionery guides from the late nineteenth century. Mrs A.B. Marshall's confectionery manuals of the 1880s, 1890s document similar whipped-sugar-and-gelatine preparations. The domestic technique using hot sugar syrup at the hard-ball stage poured into gelatine-softened whites is a standard public-domain method reproduced in English-language confectionery references.

content.text: Homemade marshmallows are lighter and better flavoured than the commercial kind. A hot sugar syrup is poured into whipped gelatine and egg whites, then beaten until thick and cool. The outside gets a thin, yielding skin from the icing sugar and cornflour coat. They toast well over a flame.

items.name: Gelatine leaves
items.prepNote: soaked in cold water for 5 minutes

content.text: Line a 20 cm square tin with lightly oiled cling film. Mix the {{icing-sugar}} and {{cornflour}} together in a small bowl. Dust the lined tin generously with this mixture.
Combine the {{granulated-sugar}}, {{glucose-syrup}}, and {{water}} in a medium saucepan. Boil to 127°C. Squeeze excess water from the bloomed {{gelatine-leaves}} (see
) and add them to the hot syrup off the heat; stir until dissolved. Leave to cool slightly while you prepare the egg whites.

Whip the {{egg-whites}} to stiff peaks in the stand mixer. With the mixer running on medium, pour the warm gelatine syrup in a thin stream down the side of the bowl. Add the {{vanilla-extract}}. Increase to high speed and whip for 8 to 10 minutes until the mixture is very thick, white, and cool to the touch.

Pour the mixture into the prepared tin. Smooth the top and dust with more of the icing sugar and cornflour mixture. Leave at room temperature for at least 4 hours to set (overnight is better). Turn out onto a surface dusted with more of the mixture and cut into cubes with an oiled knife. Toss in the remaining coating mixture.
